g.data-narration: Welcome to learning about StringBuffer and StringBuilder, the mutable string classes in Java.
The String class in Java is immutable. Every modification creates a new object, causing performance issues.
g.text: Each modification creates a NEW String object:

g.data-narration: StringBuffer and StringBuilder solve this by providing mutable character sequences that can be modified in place.
g.text: Both classes provide MUTABLE character sequences:

g.data-narration: StringBuffer is thread-safe and synchronized, introduced in Java 1.0 for multi-threaded environments.
g.text: A thread-safe, mutable sequence of characters:

g.data-narration: StringBuffer has three constructors: default 16 capacity, specified capacity, or initialize with a String.
The append() method adds content to the end, while insert() adds content at a specified position.
StringBuffer provides delete() to remove characters, reverse() to reverse the sequence, and replace() for substitution.
Capacity is the total storage allocated, while length is the actual number of characters used.
StringBuffer automatically grows when needed using the formula new capacity equals old capacity plus one times two.
StringBuilder is like StringBuffer but not thread-safe, introduced in Java 5 for better single-threaded performance.
g.text: Introduced in Java 5 - Same API but faster:

g.data-narration: The key difference is thread-safety: StringBuffer is synchronized, StringBuilder is not, making Builder faster.
Use StringBuilder for single-threaded code for speed, StringBuffer for multi-threaded code for safety, and String for immutable data.
Method chaining allows calling multiple methods in sequence because most methods return the this reference.
In summary, use String for immutable data, StringBuilder for single-threaded mutable strings, and StringBuffer for thread-safe mutable strings.
g.text: • StringBuilder: Fast, not thread-safe - use for single-threaded code
• StringBuffer: Thread-safe, synchronized - use for multi-threaded code
• capacity() = total space, length() = used space
